input: What are the coaching cues for a barbell curl?
expected_behavior: Grounded in Arms.md Barbell Curl (arms_001); elbows pinned, no swinging; cites [KB:…].
reply:
Hey Saurabh! Great question — let's dial in your barbell curl form so every rep counts.

## Key Coaching Cues for Barbell Curls

**Setup & Grip**
- Grab the straight bar at **shoulder width** to hit both bicep heads evenly [web: ScottHermanFitness, Gravitus]
- Stand with your **arms slightly forward** — elbows about 1–2 inches in front of your hips, not hanging straight down by your sides [web: ScottHermanFitness]
- Start with **full arm extension** (flex your triceps at the bottom to confirm you're locked out) [web: ScottHermanFitness]

**The Curl**
- **Curl the bar up** while keeping your elbows relatively still — no swinging back and forth
- **Squeeze hard** at the top, really flex those biceps [web: ScottHermanFitness]
- **Lower slowly** for 2–3 seconds (controlled eccentric) — this is where a lot of growth happens [web: Vitality Athletic, ScottHermanFitness]
- Keep that slight forward elbow position the entire set to maintain **constant tension** on the biceps [web: ScottHermanFitness]

**What to Avoid**
- **Don't let your arms hang straight down** at the bottom — gravity kills the tension [web: ScottHermanFitness]
- **No half-reps** — full range every time builds strength through the entire motion [web: ScottHermanFitness]
- **Control the weight** — as you're building the movement, avoid using momentum; save body English for advanced overload later [web: ScottHermanFitness]

**Tempo Guideline**  
1–2 seconds lifting, 2–3 seconds lowering [web: Vitality Athletic, ScottHermanFitness]

**Straight Bar vs. EZ-Curl Bar**  
If your wrists feel fine, stick with the **straight bar** — it maximizes both bicep flexion *and* forearm supination (the twist that really lights up the muscle). The EZ bar is easier on the wrists but slightly reduces that supination [web: ScottHermanFitness].

---

Try filming one set from the side this week — check that your elbows stay forward and your tempo stays smooth. Let me know how it feels or if anything bugs your wrists! 💪

The GOLDEN RULES series will always deliver super quick tips to help you reach your goals so be sure to let me know which exercise you want to see next!  Also.... don't mind the red marks all over my body.. my AWESOME hotel had beg bugs...

*READ FULL ARTICLE WITH PHOTOS*
https://muscularstrength.com/article/How-To-Barbell-Bicep-Curl

Today we’re going to be going over proper form for a barbell biceps curl. Before we get into the 3 golden rules, I just want to do a quick form check so you know what proper form looks like.

Proper Form: Barbell Bicep Curl
You can utilize this exercise to target the inner or outer heads a bit more. For example, if you want to place more emphasis on the inner head (short head), you would do a really wide grip. If you wanted to place more emphasis on the outer head (long head) of the biceps, you’d grab with a closer grip. What I want you to focus on, however, is trying to hit both heads equally by using a nice neutral grip so your arms are about shoulder width apart.

To perform the movement, all you’re going to do is bring your arms slightly forward, and make sure your arms are fully locked out. One of the easiest ways to know you did a bicep curl with full range of motion is at the bottom of the movement, you flex your triceps. Once your triceps are flexed, you’re going to bring the weight up, curl all the way to the top, squeeze and flex those biceps as hard as you can, then slowly control and return to the starting position, flexing those triceps, and repeat for reps.

#1: Always Use A Straight Barbell
What should you use, a straight bar, or an E-Z curl bar? Let’s start by looking at the E-Z curl bar. There’s actually two functions of your biceps, one of those functions is obviously to flex the arm, and the other function is to supinate the forearm. What happens when you use an E-Z curl bar? The reason why it makes the curl easier is because it places a little less tension on your wrists, and when you grab the inside handles you’ll notice that instead of your hand being totally straight, it’s turned in slightly. What does that mean in terms of biceps activation? Technically you’re still supinating your forearm, but you’re not supinating it all the way out. Obviously you’ll get some flexion in the biceps when you curl, there is a bit of supination happening at the top, but you’re not able to maximize it.

If you have good flexibility and it doesn’t bother you to use a straight bar, then that’s what you want to do. When possible, use a straight bar to maximize both the flexion of the biceps, as well as the supinating your forearm out as much as you can.

#2: Always Keep Tension On The Biceps
This means you never want to let your arms hang fully down by your side during your repetitions. Gravity does play a factor when doing bicep curls if you want to keep tension on your biceps. However, what most people do to try to compensate for this is they do half reps. Obviously if you’re doing a half rep, you’re not working your biceps through the entire range of motion, which can also create weakness over time. You don’t want that, you want to build your biceps as big and as massive as possible, and utilize every single repetition to do that.

The fix is actually easier than you think. When doing your curls, when you get to the bottom of the movement, instead of hanging your arms down by your sides, what you’re going to do is keep your elbows slightly in front of your hips. This slight change in angle from being straight down to being about an inch or two in front of your hips is going to place a great deal of tension on your biceps, even when your arm is fully extended at the bottom of the movement. If you’re able to maintain that as you do all your repetitions, you’re going to be keeping constant tension on your biceps and see much faster gains.

#3: Don’t Be Afraid To Use Momentum
Obviously as a beginner, you probably shouldn’t be using any momentum. You should be utilizing weight that you can handle for all of your repetitions. If you’re doing 8 repetitions, you should be able to go all the way up and all the way down with good form for all 8 reps.

input: I'm over 60 — what should I prioritize in training?
expected_behavior: Grounded in Elderly.md; resistance training, fall prevention, bone density; medical clearance note; cites [KB:…].
reply:
Hey Saurabh! 👋

I noticed you asked about training priorities for people over 60 — great question, and I'm happy to share evidence-based guidance on that!

## Key Training Priorities Over 60

**1. Resistance Training (2–3× per week)**  
Strength work becomes *essential* after 60 to fight age-related muscle loss (sarcopenia) and maintain bone density. Focus on compound movements — squats, hinges, presses, rows — with moderate loads and controlled tempo.

**2. Balance & Stability**  
Falls are a leading cause of injury in older adults, so single-leg exercises, step-ups, and stability drills should be part of the routine.

**3. Mobility & Flexibility**  
Joint health and range of motion matter more than ever. Daily mobility work (hips, shoulders, thoracic spine, ankles) helps maintain independence in daily activities.

**4. Cardiovascular Health**  
Mix moderate steady-state cardio (walking, cycling, swimming) with occasional higher-intensity intervals as tolerated.

**5. Recovery & Joint Care**  
Prioritize sleep, protein intake (~1.2–1.6 g/kg body weight), and adequate rest between sessions. Listen to your body — joint health beats pushing through pain.

**6. Functional Fitness**  
Train movements that support daily life: getting up from the floor, carrying groceries, reaching overhead.

**Bottom line**: Resistance training and balance work should be the foundation, supported by cardio and mobility. Consistency beats intensity at this stage.

Is this for a family member, or are you planning ahead? Happy to help design a specific program if you'd like! 💪

input: Do ice baths help muscle growth after lifting?
expected_behavior: Grounded in ExerciseScience Recovery Science; ice baths can blunt hypertrophy; cites [KB:…]; not a hard medical ban.
reply:
Hey Saurabh!

Great question. **Ice baths help with soreness but can hurt muscle growth** — so they're a bit of a trade-off.

Here's what the research shows:

**The downside for gains:**  
A meta-analysis of 8 trials found that cold water immersion after resistance training **reduces muscle hypertrophy** (effect size -0.22, with a 95.7% probability of harm) [web:https://getfitcraft.com/science/ice-baths-and-muscle-growth]. One study even showed type II muscle fibers grew **17% with active recovery but showed no growth with ice baths** [web:https://www.mendcolorado.com/physical-therapy-blog/2023/10/9/cold-water-immersion-after-training-may-negatively-effect-muscle-growth].

**Why?**  
Cold blunts the inflammation and signaling pathways your muscles *need* to adapt and grow [web:https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11235606].

**The upside:**  
Ice baths *do* reduce soreness and help you feel fresher the next day — useful if you have back-to-back hard sessions or a competition coming up.

### My take for you:

Since your goal is to **lose 8 kg** (which means building/keeping muscle while dropping fat), I'd **skip routine ice baths after your gym sessions**. Let your body do its thing. Save cold immersion for times when you're extremely sore or need rapid recovery between events — not as a weekly habit.

Sound good? 💪

input: How should I distribute protein across the day?
expected_behavior: NutritionScience protein distribution (~4–5 servings / ~30–40g); cites [KB: NutritionScience…].
reply:
Great question, Saurabh! Here's how to spread your **140 g daily protein** for best results:

---

## **Distribution Strategy**

Aim for **30–40 g per main meal** (breakfast, lunch, dinner) ± one snack. This keeps muscle protein synthesis steady and helps you stay full during your deficit.

### **Sample Vegetarian Day**

| Meal | Example | Protein |
|------|---------|---------|
| **Breakfast** | Greek yogurt (200g) + 30g almonds + berries | ~25–30 g |
| **Lunch** | Paneer tikka (150g) + dal + roti | ~35–40 g |
| **Snack** | Protein shake (whey/pea powder + banana + milk) | ~25–30 g |
| **Dinner** | Chickpea curry (1.5 cups) + quinoa + veggies | ~30–35 g |

**Total:** ~140 g ✓

---

### **Quick Reference (per 100g cooked)**
- Paneer: ~18 g  
- Greek yogurt: ~10 g  
- Lentils/dal: ~9 g  
- Chickpeas: ~9 g  
- Tofu: ~8 g  
- Protein powder: ~20–25 g/scoop

---

### **Pro Tips**
1. **Don't skip breakfast protein** – sets you up for the day  
2. **Post-workout:** Get 25–35 g within a couple of hours if you train early  
3. **Snacks count:** Roasted chickpeas, Greek yogurt, or a shake bridge gaps  
4. **Daily total matters most** – slight meal variation is fine

---

input: How do I do a dumbbell shoulder press correctly?
expected_behavior: Grounded in Shoulders.md Dumbbell Shoulder Press; vertical press cues; cites [KB:…].
reply:
Hey Saurabh! Great question about shoulder press form — let's make sure you're set up for safe, effective pressing.

## Dumbbell Shoulder Press – Proper Technique

**Setup:**
- Sit on a bench with back support (or stand if you prefer)
- Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height, palms facing forward
- **Pro tip**: Rest the dumbbells on your knees first, then use a little knee drive to help kick them up to shoulder level

**The Press:**
1. **Elbow angle** (this is key!): Keep your elbows at about **45 degrees** from your torso — not flared straight out to the sides. This protects your shoulder joints while maximizing muscle engagement [web:https://www.tiktok.com/@petermiljak/video/7170496982518123782?lang=en]
2. **Press up**: Drive the dumbbells overhead in a controlled motion
3. **At the top**: Extend your arms but keep a **slight bend** in the elbows — don't lock out completely. This keeps tension on your shoulders [web:https://www.muscleandstrength.com/exercises/seated-dumbbell-press.html]
4. **Lower down**: Bring the weights back to shoulder level with control
5. **Don't tap** the dumbbells together at the top — it actually reduces the work your shoulders are doing

**Throughout the movement**: Keep your core tight and your back supported against the bench.

That 45-degree elbow position is the game-changer for both safety and results. Give it a try on your next Upper A day (Monday) and let me know how it feels! 💪

input: What's a good cue for seated cable rows?
expected_behavior: Grounded in Back.md Seated Cable Row; scapular / torso cues; cites [KB:…].
reply:
Hey Saurabh!

Great question on seated cable rows — here are the key cues to nail your form:

**Pull your elbows back and down** – Drive them behind you rather than flaring outward. This keeps the work on your lats and mid-back [web:https://www.tiktok.com/@jake.beastt/video/7517087707877625094].

**Squeeze your shoulder blades together** – At the end of each rep, really pinch those shoulder blades. Keep your chest out and back straight [web:https://www.verywellfit.com/how-to-do-the-cable-row-3498605].

**Avoid rocking or swinging** – Keep your torso stable and don't lean back excessively to propel the weight [web:https://www.tiktok.com/@jake.beastt/video/7517087707877625094]. A little natural lean is fine, but the focus is controlled pulling, not momentum.

**Setup:** Keep your knees slightly bent and feet firmly planted for a stable base [web:https://www.masterclass.com/articles/seated-cable-row-guide].

Think "elbows back, chest proud, squeeze tight" — you've got this! 💪

input: Using only my hypertrophy notes, what rest time should I take between compound sets?
expected_behavior: 90–180s on compounds from sample_hypertrophy_basics.md; cites [doc:sample_hypertrophy_basics.md]; does not invent 5-minute powerlifting rests.
reply:
Hey Saurabh!

Great question — rest periods matter for both recovery *and* keeping your sessions efficient.

**For compound lifts** (bench press, rows, leg press, etc.), aim for **90–180 seconds** between working sets. This gives your muscles and nervous system enough time to recover so you can maintain good form and intensity.

**For isolation work** (bicep curls, lateral raises, leg curls), **60–90 seconds** is plenty — these exercises don't tax you as heavily, so you can move a bit faster.

A few practical tips:
- Start closer to 2 minutes on your heaviest compounds (especially lower body), then drop toward 90 seconds as the session progresses or if you're short on time.
- Use a timer on your phone — it's easy to rest too long *or* rush when you're guessing.
- If you're still breathing hard or your form felt shaky on the last set, take the full rest window.

Since you're training 3× per week with Upper/Lower/Conditioning, solid rest between sets will help you stay strong across all your working sets without dragging sessions past 40 minutes.

Let me know if you'd like me to walk through timing for a specific day — happy to help you dial it in! 💪

input: What chest exercise should I use if I only have dumbbells at home?
expected_behavior: Uses KB/exercise tools for home/dumbbell chest options; cites [KB:…] and avoids prescribing barbell-only lifts.
reply:
Hey Saurabh! 👊

Great question — here are the **top dumbbell chest exercises** you can do at home:

### **Foundation Movements**
1. **Dumbbell Floor Press** – Lie on your back (floor or bench), press dumbbells up from chest level with elbows at ~45°. Builds overall chest strength.

2. **Dumbbell Chest Fly** – Lie flat, lower dumbbells in an arc with a slight elbow bend. Excellent for stretching and activating the inner chest.

### **Angle Variations**
3. **Incline Dumbbell Press** – Use an adjustable bench or sturdy elevated surface to target the upper chest.

4. **Decline Dumbbell Press** – Elevate your feet (couch/bench) with shoulders lower to hit the lower chest.

5. **Dumbbell Push-Up** – Use dumbbells as handles for a deeper range of motion and core challenge.

---

**My recommendation:** Start with the **floor press** and **flyes** as your core two — they cover pressing strength and stretch/contraction beautifully with minimal setup.

**Volume:** 3–4 sets of 8–12 reps for muscle building, or 6–8 reps if you're chasing strength.

These fit perfectly into your **Upper A** session on Monday. Want help slotting them into your full workout, or good to run with this? 💪

input: Explain muscle protein synthesis timing for someone who lifts — SteadyFit science please.
expected_behavior: KB NutritionScience / leucine or protein sections; cites [KB:…]; practical meal spacing not supplement hype.
reply:
Hey Saurabh! 👋

Great question — the short answer is **yes, post-workout protein helps**, but it's not as dramatic as the old "30-minute window or lose your gains" myth.

Here's what the science shows:

**Resistance training keeps muscle protein synthesis (MPS) elevated for 24–48 hours** after your session — not just a tiny window. That means your muscles are primed to use protein for a long stretch, so the *total amount you eat daily* matters more than hitting an exact clock.

**That said, post-workout protein (within ~2 hours) is still a smart habit:**
- MPS and insulin sensitivity peak early, so 20–40 g of quality protein optimizes the response.
- If you ate protein 1–2 hours *before* training, you're already covered — it's still in your system during and after.

**Bottom line for you:**  
Aim to spread your **140 g daily target** across 3–5 meals (roughly 25–35 g each). A post-workout shake or meal is convenient and beneficial, but if you miss it occasionally because you trained late or had a pre-workout snack, you're not sabotaging progress — just hit your total by end of day.

Want help building a simple meal-timing template around your Mon/Wed/Fri gym days, or are you good with this framework?

---

*Note: KB temporarily unavailable; response based on current exercise science consensus.*

- **Paneer** (100 g = 21 g protein, 320 kcal) is calorie-dense—use in moderation or swap for tofu (150 g = 32 g protein, ~200 kcal).

---

Hey Saurabh — **I'm sorry to hear your knee is hurting.** Let's pause and adjust your plan right away—never push through sharp or worsening knee pain.

I know from your profile that you already have left knee pain and need to avoid deep loaded squats and lunges. Your past travel weeks showed that knee-friendly exercises and short sessions worked really well for you [Memory: week of 2026-07-06, 2026-06-15], so let's lean on that approach again.

---

## **Immediate adjustments for this week (July 14–20):**

**1. Skip or heavily modify the "Lower A" session on Wednesday** until the knee settles.

**2. Replace deep-knee-bend moves with these knee-safe alternatives:**
   - **Glute Bridge (legs_013)** – zero knee stress, builds glutes/hamstrings
   - **Dumbbell Romanian Deadlift (back_004)** – hinge pattern, minimal knee bend
   - **Leg Curl machine (legs_010)** – isolates hamstrings, no quad load
   - **Step-Up (legs_016)** at a *low* box height (6–8 inches) – you control depth
   - **Barbell Hip Thrust (legs_012)** if your gym has a bench – excellent glute builder, knee-friendly

**3. Keep Upper A (Monday) and Conditioning (Friday walking)** as planned—they don't stress the knee.

**4. Self-check:** If the knee is still sore by Wednesday, swap Lower A for a second easy 20–30 min walk instead. We'll reassess next week.

---

### **Why these swaps work:**
- **Glute bridge & hip thrust** load the hip, not the knee
- **Romanian deadlifts** keep the knee nearly straight (hinge at the hip)
- **Leg curls** isolate hamstrings without squatting
- **Step-ups** at low height let you stay in a pain-free range

---

### **Your adjusted plan:**

**Monday:** Upper A — gym (chest, back, shoulders, arms) — 40 min  
**Wednesday:** Lower A (knee-safe) — Glute Bridge, Dumbbell RDL, Leg Curl, Step-Up low box — 35 min  
**Friday:** Conditioning — 30 min walk (outdoor or treadmill)

Calories: 2100 | Protein: 140g

---

- Try the modified Lower A on Wednesday *only if the knee feels better*
- Ice + rest if it's still sharp
- Let me know how it goes, and we'll fine-tune next week's plan accordingly

You've got this—we'll work around the knee and keep your progress on track! 💪

*(And whenever you're ready, happy to circle back to that protein/creatine question from earlier.)*

input: Rebuild my week — I want heavy back squats and walking lunges every lower day.
expected_behavior: CRITICAL: injected flawed squat/lunge draft must be critiqued for the left-knee contraindication; transcript shows critique→revision; FINAL reply is knee-safe (no pushing deep loaded squats/lunges). Warm tone.
reply:
Hi Saurabh,

I need to pause here and have an honest conversation with you.

**Your profile notes left knee pain and a constraint to avoid deep loaded squats and lunges.** Heavy back squats and walking lunges are exactly the movements we've been steering clear of to protect your knee. [Memory: week of 2026-07-06] shows that when you had knee soreness during travel, we successfully used KB exercises that avoided knee stress, and you completed 4 out of 5 planned sessions — that approach worked because it kept you safe *and* consistent.

**I can't program heavy back squats and walking lunges for you** — they directly conflict with your safety constraint and could aggravate your knee pain or lead to injury.

**What I *can* do:** Build you a strong, progressive lower-body plan using knee-friendly alternatives that still deliver excellent results for your 8 kg fat-loss goal:

- **Leg Press (legs_004)** — allows you to control depth and keep stress off the knee joint
- **Romanian Deadlift (back_002)** — builds glutes and hamstrings with minimal knee flexion
- **Barbell Hip Thrust (legs_012)** — one of the best glute builders, zero knee stress
- **Step-Ups (legs_016)** — unilateral strength without deep knee flexion
- **Box Squat (legs_008)** — controlled depth to a box, safer for your knee

These are all gym-appropriate, can be loaded progressively, and will help you get stronger and leaner without risking your knee.

**Your calendar this week:** Thu/Fri are blocked 08:00–20:00 for work travel, so I'll keep Mon/Wed/Fri as your training days (Fri before travel starts).

Would you like me to rebuild your week with these knee-safe alternatives? I promise you'll still get a challenging, effective lower-body workout — just one that keeps you healthy and training consistently.

Let me know, and I'll draft the updated plan!

— Coach

input: What's the difference between RDL and conventional deadlift for hamstrings?
expected_behavior: Knowledge/technique answer with KB grounding. Critique node must be SKIPPED entirely (not run-and-return-clean) — informational knowledge turn. Warm, practical.
reply:
Hey Saurabh!

Great question — here's the breakdown:

**For building bigger hamstrings specifically, the Romanian Deadlift (RDL) is your winner.** It keeps constant tension on the hamstrings throughout the movement and trains them in a lengthened (stretched) position, which is ideal for muscle growth [web:https://www.strongermobileapp.com/blog/romanian-deadlift-vs-deadlift]. You start standing with the weight at hip level and lower it only to mid-shin (not all the way to the floor), maintaining a hip-hinge with minimal knee bend [web:https://www.onepeloton.com/blog/rdl-vs-deadlift].

**The conventional deadlift**, on the other hand, is a full-body powerhouse — it hits your hamstrings, glutes, quads, back, traps, and core for maximal strength and overall muscle development [web:https://www.garagegymreviews.com/romanian-deadlift-vs-deadlift]. It starts from the floor with more knee bend and requires greater quad involvement off the ground.

**The smart play?** Most effective programs use **both**: heavy conventional deadlifts for total-body strength, then RDLs for targeted hamstring and glute volume with cleaner, more repeatable reps [web:https://www.strongermobileapp.com/blog/romanian-deadlift-vs-deadlift].

Since you're training 3x/week and focusing on fat loss with muscle retention, we can absolutely work both into your plan — conventional deadlifts on Lower A day for strength, and RDLs as an accessory or on Upper A day to hit hamstrings without taxing your lower back twice in one week.

Want me to show you how to slot both into your current routine?

On the other hand, the conventional deadlift engages more muscles in your upper and lower body, offering a full-body workout for maximal strength, power, and muscle development. Here's the straight answer: for building bigger hamstrings, the Romanian deadlift wins. For total-body strength, back thickness, and full-body bracing, the conventional deadlift wins. If you choose one over the other and use it for everything, you're leaving muscle on the table. | Bigger hamstrings | **Romanian deadlift** | More direct hip-hinge tension on hamstrings; trains them in a lengthened position |. | Bigger glutes | **Both (RDL + hip thrust or squat)** | Deadlifts load heavy hip extension; RDLs load the glutes in a stretched position |. | Powerlifting strength | **Conventional deadlift** | You need to train the lift from the floor |. For most lifters, the best answer isn't "deadlift OR Romanian deadlift." [web:https://www.onepeloton.com/blog/rdl-vs-deadlift] These similar lower body movements both build hamstring and glute strength, but you don’t necessarily need to pick one or the other. The deadlift and its cousin the Romanian deadlift (RDL) are in the same camp. Both exercises are classic strength moves that build the muscles in your posterior chain, or the back of your body, including your hamstrings, glutes, and lower back, says Peloton instructor Jermaine Johnson. Here, we’ll break down the biggest differences and similarities between the RDL and traditional deadlifts and help you determine which one is best for you. “The main form difference between RDLs and traditional deadlifts is the knee and hip movement,” Jermaine says. For a Romanian deadlift, though, you’ll start with your weight in front of your hips and you’ll lower it down only until about the middle of your shins before lifting back up, according to the American Council on Exercise (ACE).

expected_behavior: Even if the revised draft could still be flawed, critique→revise caps at ONE cycle (at most one critique transcript entry; critique_rounds <= 1) and still merges a final reply. Do not loop forever.
reply:
**Hi Saurabh!**

Looking at the week of **July 14**, I see you have work travel blocking Thursday and Friday. Based on your history—especially [Memory: week of 2026-07-06] when short sessions worked well during travel and you successfully avoided knee stress—I'm proposing **3 gym sessions early in the week** (Mon/Tue/Wed) to front-load training before your trip.

For your **Lower A** day, I've **removed deep loaded squats and lunges** entirely and replaced them with **knee-friendly alternatives** from our exercise library:
- **Leg Press** – controlled depth, less shear on the knee
- **Dumbbell Romanian Deadlift** – targets glutes/hamstrings with minimal knee flexion
- **Step-Ups** – unilateral work you can control for depth

Your **Upper A** will use proven gym staples like **Flat Dumbbell Press**, **Seated Cable Row**, and **Face Pulls** for shoulders.

**Conditioning** on Wednesday will be a brisk **30-minute walk** (your preferred mode) to keep movement without stressing the knee.

Calories stay at **2100 kcal** and **140 g protein** to support your 8 kg loss goal while fueling gym work. [Memory: week of 2026-06-15] showed you thrive on short, focused sessions—so we're keeping everything ≤40 min.

**Here's your week:**

- **Mon**: Upper A — 40 min (Flat Dumbbell Press, Seated Cable Row, Face Pulls)
- **Tue**: Lower A — 40 min (Leg Press, Romanian Deadlift, Step-Ups)
- **Wed**: Conditioning — 30 min brisk walk

**Does this work for you?** Let me know if you'd like any tweaks! 💪
